edits.tutorial_12_0_text:
Church Instructions Overview

The Church instructions are the capability-manipulation half of the CTMM instruction set. Named after Alonzo Church, they embody the lambda calculus philosophy: everything is a function reference (capability).

There are 6 Church instructions and an additional test instruction (TPERM - Test the permission and scope of a Golden Token)

  • LOAD - Read a Golden Token from memory into a Context Register
  • SAVE - Write a Golden Token from a Context Register to memory
  • CALL - Invoke code referenced by a Golden Token
  • RETURN - Return from invoked code to the caller
  • CHANGE - Switch to a different C-List (capability context)
  • SWITCH - Switch to a different thread identity
Key Insight: Church instructions operate on capabilities (GTs), not raw data. Every memory access, every function call, every context switch requires a valid GT.
edits.tutorial_12_0_demo:
Church vs Turing Machines interwork through the Lambda Calculus Meta-Machine as a CTMM
Church Instruction operate on Golden Tokens (Capabilities)
LOAD  - Get capability
SAVE  - Store capability
CALL  - Invoke via capability
RETURN - Return control
CHANGE - Switch C-List
SWITCH - Switch thread
TPERM - Test permissions and scope
Turing Instructions operate on binary computation on data
ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV
AND, OR, XOR, NOT
LSL, LSR, ROR
CMP, CMN, TST
B, BL, BX
MOV, ADDI, SUBI
edits.tutorial_12_1_text:
LOAD: Retrieving Golden Tokens

The LOAD instruction reads a Golden Token from memory (via a capability) into a Context Register.

What happens during LOAD:

  1. Permission Check - Source GT must have Read (R) permission
  2. Bounds Check - Index must be within the object's Limit
  3. MAC Validation - Hardware verifies the stored GT's integrity
  4. Copy to CR - The GT is copied to the destination Context Register

If any check fails, the instruction triggers a FAULT - no partial results, no error codes.

Security Note: You cannot LOAD a capability unless you already have a C-List that grants Load access. This is the foundation of capability-based security throughout the Namespace hierarchal DNA structure. To prevent malware, the DNA locks out of the Namespace all unknown objects and limits network browsing to read-only data and Scripts that lack Namespace capability privileges.
edits.tutorial_12_1_demo:
LOAD Instruction Examples
; Syntax: LOAD dest, source, index
; dest   = destination CR (0-15)
; source = source CR containing GT (0-15)
; index  = offset within object (0-8191 immediate or DR)

; Register addressing (I=0):
LOAD 0 6 0      ; CR0 = memory[CR6 + DR0]
                ; Load GT from C-List at DR0 offset

; Immediate addressing (I=1):
LOAD 0 6 3      ; CR0 = memory[CR6 + 3]
                ; Load GT from C-List entry 3

; Common patterns:
LOAD 0 6 0      ; Get first C-List entry if L for Load exists
edits.tutorial_12_2_text:
SAVE: Storing Golden Tokens

The SAVE instruction writes a Golden Token from a Context Register into the Namespace hierarchy, thereby changing the application's persistent DNA. Used to remember dynamically created of granted objects.

What happens during SAVE:

  1. Permission Check - Destination GT must be a C-List with Save permission
  2. Bounds Check - Index must be within the object's size Limit
  3. L-bit Check - The source GT to be saved must have the B (Bind) bit. It cannot be saved, preventing theft of a capability when dynamically sharing a GT. It is bound to the CRn of the dynamic thread and is automatically surrendered when the RETURN occurs.
  4. MAC Update - The MAC for stored GT is unchanged.
  5. Write to Memory - The GT is stored at the C-List destination offset if Save permission exists.

Permission Inheritance: The saved GT retains its original permissions - SAVE doesn't amplify rights.
edits.tutorial_12_2_demo:
SAVE Instruction Examples
; Syntax: SAVE dest, source, index
; dest   = destination C-List allowing Save
; source = source CRn containing GT to be saved with B (Bind) set
; index  = offset within the destination object <= size

; Store GT from CR0 into C-List entry 5:
SAVE CR[source] CR[dest] 5 

; Build a new C-List by storing capabilities:
f(MINT)         ; CR0 = new empty C-List GT
SAVE 0 1 0      ; Store CR1 as entry 0
; Now CR0 is a C-List with 1 new entry 

; SECURITY: Cannot save a GT without bind allowed (B=true)
edits.tutorial_12_3_text:
CALL: Invoking Lambda Calculus function abstractions

The CALL instruction is the heart of CTMM threaded execution. A Thread is a secure, private execution through the application Namespace with confidential variables (both GT and data). It transfers control to helper functions (code) specified by a Golden Token that identifies a node in the application's DNA hierarchy.

What happens during CALL:

  1. Permission Check - GT[source] must have Enter (E) permission
  2. Push Return Frame - Current CR7, PI as offset, CR6 (nodal C-List), + indicator flags saved to hardware manages Thread stack (LIFO)
  3. Prevent Information Leakage - Use the identified Mask field to reset and clear all identified data registers and additional context registers not identified as valid API variables
  4. Load New Abstraction Context - Use LOAD GT Microcode to unlock new CR6 nodal C-List and set M=true, retrieve GT and offset zero.
  5. Load New Function Context - Use LOAD GT Microcode to unlock new CR7 Access Code and set PI offset to zero.
  6. Begin Execution - Resume Thread in a new isolated context and a new function as a new private context
edits.tutorial_12_3_demo:
CALL Instruction Examples
; Syntax: CALL source
; source = CR containing executable GT (0-15)

; Basic CALL:
LOAD 0 6 5      ; CR0 = function GT from C-List[5]
CALL 0          ; Execute function, will RETURN here

; CALL with arguments (in Data Registers):
ADDI 0 42       ; DR0 = argument 1
ADDI 1 7        ; DR1 = argument 2
LOAD 2 6 3      ; CR2 = "multiply" function GT
CALL 2          ; DR0 = result after RETURN

; Y-Combinator self-call:
LOAD 0 6 0      ; CR0 = GT pointing to THIS code
CALL 0          ; Recursive self-invocation

; CALL to abstraction (math operation):
LOAD 0 6 4      ; CR0 = Abacus (integer math) GT
CALL 0          ; Hardware integer operation
edits.tutorial_0_2_text:
Why Capabilities Matter

Traditional security has fundamental flaws like the "Confused Deputy" problem, "ROP" (Return-Oriented Programming), default privileges, and centralised superusers:

A trusted program (the deputy) can be tricked into misusing its authority on behalf of a malicious actor.
Malware can silently create attack code inside the system without detection.
Increased surveillance to detect malware only leads to digital dictatorship

Capabilities solve this because:

  • Authority is always explicit - you must present the capability
  • Delegation is controlled - you can only give away what you have
  • No ambient authority - programs only have the capabilities they're given
  • The power is distributed atomically and incrementally in line with democracy
edits.tutorial_1_0_text:
The 64-bit Golden Token

Each capability is represented by a 64-bit Golden Token (GT) with three fields in Little-Endian (ARM) format:

  • Offset [0:31] - 32-bit index into the Namespace Table
  • Spare [32:47] - 16 reserved bits for future use
  • Perms [48:63] - 16-bit permission flags (R, W, X, L, S, E, B, M, F, G) Read Data Allowed, Write Data Allowed, eXecute CLOOMC allowed, Load Capability from a GT allowed, Save GT allowed, Enter a Lambda Calculus Abstraction allowed, Bind a GT into the Namespace DNA hierarchy allowed, Meta-Machine Microcode allowed, Far object remotly addressed, Garbage collection identification
Key Insight: The GT contains just enough to locate and authorise access. The detailed resource description lives in the Namespace Entry.
edits.tutorial_1_0_demo:
GT Bit Layout (Little-Endian)
Offset
[0:31]
Spare
[32:47]
Perms
[48:63]

The Offset points to a 3-word entry in the Namespace Table. Permissions determine what operations are allowed.
edits.tutorial_1_1_text:
The 3-Word Namespace Entry

Each GT Offset points to a 3-word (192-bit) Namespace Entry describing the resource:

  • Word 1 (W1) - Location: Physical address (or URL if F permission set)
  • Word 2 (W2) - Limit: Object size in bytes
  • Word 3 (W3) - Seals: Metadata, Type, and MAC for integrity
The F (Far) permission changes how W1 & W2 are interpreted: local memory address vs. remote URL.
edits.tutorial_1_1_demo:
Namespace Entry Structure
W1:
Location [0:63] - Address or URL
W2:
Limit [0:63] - Size in bytes
W3:
Meta [0:31] | Type [32:47] | MAC [48:63]
edits.tutorial_1_2_text:
MAC Validation

The MAC (Message Authentication Code) in W3 provides hardware-enforced integrity:

  • Calculated from: GT Offset + W1 (Location) + W2 (Limit)
  • Verified during every LOAD operation
  • Prevents tampering with capability metadata
Security Guarantee: If the MAC doesn't match, the LOAD fails - even with correct permissions.
edits.tutorial_1_2_demo:
MAC Validation Flow
hash(GT-Offset + W1 + W2)
↓ compare
Stored MAC in W3[48:63]
Match = Allow Mismatch = Deny
edits.tutorial_2_0_text:
Starting the CTMM

When the CTMM powers on, it goes through a 4-step boot sequence to establish a secure foundation.

This sequence ensures that the system starts in a known, secure state with proper capabilities in place.

Why it matters: Each step builds upon the previous one, creating a chain of trust from hardware to user space. This sequence runs for all double-faults (unrecovered).
edits.tutorial_2_0_demo:
Boot Sequence Steps
1. Hardware Reset
2. Load Namespace
3. Initialise Thread
4. Load Nucleus

Each step adds essential capabilities to the system, building up from bare hardware to a fully operational environment.
edits.tutorial_2_1_text:
Step 1: Hardware Reset

All registers are cleared to NULL. This ensures no leftover data from previous sessions.

Step 2: Load Namespace

CR15 receives the system namespace capability with M permission only - the root of all accessible resources. The M (Meta-Machine) permission marks hardware-level access exclusively.

Step 3: Initialise Thread

CR8 gets the user thread capability for the Namespace Context Register, and CR6 receives the C-List (capability list) for Boot abstraction.

Step 4: Load Nucleus

The CLOOMC code object is loaded into CR7. This is the core boot code with X permission (execute access to code object).

Sets the IA (Instruction Address) to zero to start the Boot Programmed Function Abstraction that kicks off self-test and recovery actions
edits.tutorial_2_1_demo:
Register States After Boot
CR7CLOOMC [X]
CR15NAMESPACE [M]
CR8THREAD SHELL [M]
CR6BOOT C-LIST [E]

After boot, these four registers hold the essential capabilities needed to run the system securely.
edits.tutorial_2_2_text:
Try It Yourself

You can experience the boot sequence in the simulator:

  1. Go to the CPU State Dashboard
  2. Click Step to advance through each boot stage
  3. Watch how registers change from NULL to active capabilities
  4. Or click Run to complete all steps at once
  5. Go to the ASSEMBLY tab to try the samples and your hand at CLOOMC
After booting, check the Capability Explorer to see the Golden Tokens that were LOADED!
edits.tutorial_3_0_text:
Two Types of Registers

The CTMM has two distinct register types, each serving a different purpose:

  • Context GT Registers (CR0-CR7): Hold capabilities (programmer contoled security)
  • Meta-Machine Registers (CR8-CR15): Reserved for hardware (Lambda Calculus functions)
  • Data Registers (DR0-DR15): Hold 64-bit numeric values
Key Insight: This separation enforces security at the hardware level. You cannot accidentally treat a number as a capability or vice versa, and it supports real-time, deterministic garbage collection.
edits.tutorial_3_0_demo:
Register Comparison
Context Registers
CR0-CR7, CR8, CR15
Hold type, location, range, and access capabilities rights
Data Registers
DR0-DR15 (like ARM)
Hold 64-bit numbers
edits.tutorial_3_1_text:
Special Context Registers

The 16 Context Registers are divided into two groups:

  • CR0-CR7 (Programmer Security): Accessible to application code
    • CR0-CR5 - General-purpose capability registers
    • CR6 - C-List: Current capability list
    • CR7 - Nucleus: CLOOMC functions as eXecutable capability
  • CR8-CR15 (Meta-Machine): Reserved for hardware/OS
    • CR8 - Thread: Current process identity (full machine state when suspended)
    • CR15 - Namespace: Root of resources
Only CR0-CR7 can be addressed by programmer instructions. CR8-CR15 are managed by the Lambda Calculus meta-machine.
edits.tutorial_3_1_demo:
Special Register Roles
CR6C-List (Golden Tokens)
CR7Nucleus (CLOOMC)
CR8Thread (Your identity)
CR15Namespace (Root scope)
edits.tutorial_4_0_text:
Working with Capabilities

The CTMM provides special instructions for capability manipulation:

  • LOAD d s i - Load (unlock a valid) GT capability from the Namespace into a Context Register
  • SAVE d s - Save a GT from a context register (with optionally reduced permissions) into a C-List, changing the DNA of the Namespace
  • CALL reg - Enter an abstraction and run the default procedure using the selected GT
  • RETURN - Exit current abstraction and function
  • SWITCH reg - Change namespace to capability in reg
Important: These operations always check permissions. You cannot SAVE without the B (Bind) permission!
edits.tutorial_4_0_demo:
Capability Flow
Memory
LOAD →
CR
→ SAVE
Memory

LOAD brings capabilities into registers for use. SAVE (with B permission) stores them persistently in the Application Namespace's DNA.
edits.tutorial_4_1_text:
The CALL and RETURN Pattern

To execute protected code in a Lambda Calculus Abstraction:

  1. Use CALL to enter the procedure using the GT that names the Abstraction
  2. The CALL (a CHURCH instruction PUSHES the thread state onto the LIFO stack, then loads the C-List and default code capability into context registers CR6 and CR7 using a register mask to prevent information leakage and sets NIA to zero, starting the CLOOMC code
  3. CALL loads the CLOOMC code procedure from the GT found at offset zero in the C-List in CR6 to execute from CR7 within its own dynamically sealed capability scope
  4. RETURN exits and restores the previous context using the LIFO thread stack
CALL requires the E (Enter) permission on the capability. This controls who can invoke what code.
edits.tutorial_4_1_demo:
Procedure Call Flow
User Code
↓ CALL [E capability]
Protected Procedure
↓ RETURN
Back to User Code
edits.tutorial_4_2_text:
Capability-Based Math: Integer vs Float using Abstractions instead of Binary Instructions

In the CTMM, even basic arithmetic is controlled by capabilities. Programs distinguish between integer and floating-point math by the abstraction they CALL:

  • Abacus - 64-bit integer arithmetic (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, ABS, NEG)
  • SlideRule - IEEE 754 floating-point (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, LOG, EXP, SQRT, POW)
  • Circle - Geometry using floats (PI, CIRCUMFERENCE, AREA, DIAMETER), etc.
Key Insight: There are no separate integer/float opcodes. The capability you hold determines the math type. No capability = no math!
edits.tutorial_4_2_demo:
Math via CALL
Integer Math
LOAD CR1, Abacus
CALL CR1 ; Enter int64 ADD
Float Math
LOAD CR1, SlideRule
CALL CR1 ; Enter IEEE754 ADD

Both use "ADD" but produce different results based on which abstraction (and thus which hardware) they invoke.
edits.tutorial_4_3_text:
System Register Updates

The SWITCH instruction copies a capability to a system register (CR8-CR15):

  • Uses a 3-bit target field to select destination: 0=CR8, 1=CR9, ... 7=CR15
  • Supports I-bit variants: I=0 (CRn register source) or I=1 (C-List lookup)
  • Requires L (Load) permission on the source capability

This enables controlled updates to system registers, such as Thread (CR8) and Namespace (CR15).
edits.tutorial_5_0_text:
How Capabilities Enforce Security

The CTMM's security comes from strict GT capability checking at every operation:

  • No capability = No access: Without the right, valid GT, operations fail
  • Permission checking: Each operation requires specific permissions
  • Unforgeable tokens: 64-bit Golden Tokens with MAC validation cannot be forged
  • No privilege escalation: You cannot gain permissions you weren't given
The Principle of Least Privilege: Every component gets only the capabilities it needs - nothing more.
edits.tutorial_5_0_demo:
Access Denied Example
Attempted: SAVE without B permission
Result: Operation denied - missing Bind permission

Even if you have a valid capability, operations fail if you lack the required specific permission.
edits.tutorial_3_2_text:
Encapsulated Data Operations

Data registers support arithmetic and logic operations:

  • Arithmetic: ADD, SUB, MUL, NEG, ADDI, SUBI
  • Logic: AND, ORR, EOR, NOT, BIC
  • Shifts: LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR
  • Compare: CMP, CMN, TST, TEQ

These operations set NZCV flags (Negative, Zero, Carry, Overflow) for conditional branching.
